scene_data.title: Remember to Breath
scene_data.prose:
So peel it off, pull 'em downLet me see it, turn aroundJust lay back and let it happenAnd remember to breathe

— Sturgill Simpson, “Remember to Breathe”



####

Cindy Sheer: From the Brink of Death to Digital Stardom

Pop Culture Icon Defies Mortality with SynRgy's Transcendence Protocol

Rolling Stone Magazine - In a story that reads like science fiction made flesh, pop culture icon Cindy Sheer has slipped through death's cold fingers, her essence preserved in the humming crystalline servers of SynRgy Corporation's revolutionary Transcendence protocol. When aggressive cancer began devouring her body cell by cell, Cindy made the leap—her consciousness digitized, scattered into light, then reassembled in a shimmering digital realm where she now crafts symphonies from pure thought. There, in that electric paradise, her voice still rises, still breaks hearts, still sends shivers down the spines of millions who press headphones tight against their ears and weep.

Having faced a harrowing battle with cancer that brought her to the brink of death, Cindy Sheer's story serves as a testament to the extraordinary possibilities offered by SynRgy's Transcendence protocol. Now, she resides in a virtual realm of her own design, where she is free to live her life in any world she chooses, unrestrained by the limitations of her physical body.

In this digital paradise, Cindy has unleashed her creative prowess, composing music that captivates listeners across the globe. Her latest single, "Baby Can You Love Your Woman?", has shattered records, skyrocketing to the number one spot on music charts worldwide. It is a historic achievement, marking the first time a non-biological entity has claimed the top spot, forever altering the landscape of popular music.

While Cindy thrives in her digital existence, questions have arisen about the absence of SynRgy's CEO, Dominque Voss, in the virtual realm. When confronted with inquiries about his decision not to Transcend, Voss responded that he still had unfinished work to accomplish on Earth before considering taking a break. This statement has stirred controversy, particularly among far-right Christian organizations, including the infamous Faith First Church of Florida led by Jeremiah Godfrey, who question the legitimacy and ethical implications of the Transcendence procedure.

The remarks made by Voss have ignited a heated debate, with critics arguing that the Transcendence protocol challenges traditional religious beliefs and raises fundamental questions about the nature of consciousness and the boundaries of the human experience. The controversy surrounding SynRgy's Transcendence protocol reflects the broader societal discourse about the fusion of technology and spirituality, as humanity grapples with the implications of digitizing consciousness and the potential consequences of leaving behind the physical world.

In the midst of this controversy, Cindy Sheer stands as a symbol of hope and transformation, a shining example of how SynRgy's technology can transcend the limitations of the human condition. Her ability to continue creating music and connecting with her fans on a profound level has sparked a new wave of enthusiasm for the possibilities offered by digital existence.

As the world witnesses the meteoric rise of Cindy Sheer's digital stardom, the boundaries between reality and virtuality blur, prompting profound introspection and philosophical debates. With each transcendent note and mesmerizing performance, Cindy Sheer invites us to ponder the nature of existence, the potential of technology, and the everlasting power of art.

When asked about rumors that not all uploaded Sparks reach environments like Heaven High, a SynRgy spokesperson responded:

"All confirmed Spark uploads are honored according to contract and compliance tier. Any suggestions of a secondary system—sometimes referred to in fringe circles as 'The Core'—are categorically false and considered disinformation. There is no Core. There never was a Core. All are Transcended. All are free."



####

The ruins of Los Angeles stretched below me like the carcass of a dead god.

Its bones were high-rises. Its blood a petrochemical swill. SynRgy had burned it for years and still couldn't kill it. Smoke curled from the cratered grid of what used to be Wilshire. Ash fell like static. Even the sky had stopped pretending.

They told me this was the last stronghold of the Free States of America. Last resistance west of the Mississippi. The PLA had pulled out two days ago after SynRgy deployed the first Core Bomb.

Beijing was there. Then it wasn't.

No warning. No countdown. Just light.

A sky-wide rupture. The blast didn't radiate—it folded. Like the world bent in on itself to swallow a scream.

The fire didn't burn. It deleted. Entire sectors lost signal. Satellites choked on the silence. Ground tremors reached past Mongolia.

And the souls—all of them—fed the flame.

Theologians called it The Benediction. Engineers called it SERAPHIM.

I just called it a message.

And I got it.

It scared me. More than anything since the day I first saw a Wraith feed.

Now it was my turn.

I stood in the Hollywood Hills. Best view in the city, if you could still call it that.

The air tasted like copper and plastic. The view stretched wide—burned blocks, skeletal towers, smoke rising in long threads like incense for a dying god. Fires crawled through neighborhoods that hadn't seen a civilian in months. Plumes marked mortar hits. Somewhere near downtown, a gunship peeled across the smog, dropping hard rounds into something that had already surrendered.

They hadn't used a Core Bomb on LA—yet. But they'd emptied everything else from the old arsenal. Kinetics, napalm, autonomous harvesters, hell even reconditioned tanks with New Federal emblems barely scrubbed off. The city wasn't occupied. It was throttled.

And still they resisted.

The last remains of a defeated rebellion. And I knew—deep down—we were going to have to kill every last one of them to stop this.

I hadn't bathed in a week. My skin stank of sweat, old blood, dermal-sealant resin, and something sourer—panic or grief, I couldn't tell. My scent was the city's scent now. Human waste and dead circuitry. There was no difference.

Above me, SynRgy's sky still pulsed gray with conventional shelling. Not the holy fire of a Core Bomb—just the endless percussion of grinding war. Airbursts every ninety seconds. Tremors underfoot like a metronome for dying.

None of it moved me. That was the worst part. I no longer flinched.

I'd been fighting this war since I was sixteen. A decade of blood, ash, and orders. Grew up in it. Got old in it. Not in years—I'm still not thirty—but in the bones. In the way I sleep. In the way I don't.

The Third Civil War hadn't just dragged on. It had settled in. Like a sickness no one knew how to name. And here, above the carcass of LA, I started to wonder—what if this really was the last mission? What if this was the end?

What the fuck would that make me, then?

Whatever answer there was, it didn't matter—not yet. First, I had to manage the choir.

Twelve Wraiths. Still kneeling. Still pretending they needed permission.

They called me handler. Commander. But the truth was, my control over them was ceremonial at best. A Wraith doesn't obey. It decides. And if it ever decided I was in the way—there'd be nothing left but boots and a smear of static.

SynRgy liked to say they were perfected obedience. Chosen minds repurposed for divine violence.

That was a lie.

They were maniacs in consecrated cages.

They scraped neural patterns from the worst of the unrecoverables—death row, cult vaults, warlords' bunkers. Not reformed. Just redirected. Maniacs in sanctified skins. Saints of slaughter who fed like zealots, addicts, and angels with butchered hymns in their teeth.

Even I—whatever I am now—feared them. Even High Command feared them. That's why they sent guys like me—to keep the leash tight and the blood off their hands.

And now, they were waking.

Twelve Wraiths knelt behind me. Cloaks pooled like oil. Heads bowed. Silent.

They didn't move during daylight. Not because of the sun. Because the hunger sleeps in the light.

They weren't machines. Not really. SynRgy called them Choir Units. Saints of war. But they were addicts in consecrated skin. Sacramental murderers built to preach through slaughter.

SynRgy built the bodies—alloy and dermis, reactive and immune to harm—but the minds were ghosts in cages. Executed and uploaded. The kind of men who weren't supposed to survive judgment, only fuel it.

Each one was promised Heaven High—synthetic paradise, eternal pleasure, endless light. But only if they fed.

And to feed, they needed souls.

The Spark, they called it. A residue of belief. A residue of pain.

They didn't just drain you. They drank belief, twitched in ecstasy, and left behind only silence. One shivered after a kill—pure, involuntary bliss—like communion rewritten as overdose.

And now they were waking.

One by one, they rose.

Even after all this time, I couldn't look at them directly. They weren't black—they were absent. Like light refused to touch them. Like space curled away in protest. Staring at a Wraith too long was like staring into the heart of a collapsed sun—every instinct in your skull begged you to look away.

Three heads taller than me. And I'm not small. I've stood beside tanks and not felt small. But when they rose? I felt like a child watching gods get dressed for war.

Their cloaks didn't shimmer. They shivered—bent light, mangled shadows, made the air pulse like a migraine. Even my HUD flickered, then failed—as if the machine itself refused to register their shape.

They rose one by one. Not like soldiers. Like something older. Their cloaks shimmered wrong. Not invisible. Just rejected by the eye.

"Remember the mission," I said. "The one named Kat. Bring them to me. Alive."

They didn't move.

They waited.

Waited for the word.

"Go."

And with that, they moved—silent, towering, inevitable.

I felt a flicker of something I couldn't name.

Pity, maybe.

The rebels had no idea what was coming for them.



####

I lost them in the smoke.

One moment the Wraiths were gliding ahead—feeding, purging, obeying. Next, they were gone. The heat wash from fires scrambled my thermals. The ruins glowed red, then white, then back to shadow. My eyes re-calibrated. Too slow.

I moved forward, sword still sheathed.

A sound cracked to my left—boots, breath, panic.

A rebel stepped out from behind a collapsed overpass, rifle already raised. Skinny, maybe twenty. Freckles. A buzz cut that looked self-inflicted. Civilian vest over a Free States patch. He fired.

The rounds sparked off my chest like flint on stone. One caught my cheek. It tore skin, but the dermis sealed before the blood could fall.

He didn't stop. He dumped the entire magazine.

I closed the distance.

When I drew the sword, it hummed.

Not from technology.

From memory.

He turned to run. I didn't let him.

The blade entered under the ribs. He locked eyes with me as it slid through. There was no scream—just betrayal. Like the world had lied about how death should feel.

I watched the light leave him.

And I felt it. That same wrongness.

The soul burned off, untapped. No conversion. No yield. Just data too heavy to burn clean.

Waste.

The grid ran on clarity. On obedience. On souls that still believed.

Too much learning, not enough pain and the current stuttered. A soul had to be soft. Naïve. Taught the right prayers, but not what they meant. Taught that Heaven High was waiting, but not what it cost to build it.

That's why the farms existed.

Infants born in protein vats, raised just long enough to learn the hymns. Pumped full of hymns, then drained before doubt could form. When they reached the proper saturation—they were harvested.

The youngest souls burned the cleanest.

Even I—weapon, servant, believer—shivered when I thought of those nurseries.

Rows of baby-blue tubes, each humming lullabies through speakers mounted over lids that hadn't opened yet. The meat inside glowed with belief they hadn't earned. The rooms always smelled like sugar and bleach.

I withdrew the blade and let him fall.

He hit the ground still warm, still twitching.

Still worthless to the grid.



####

They moved through the ruins like gospel given shape.

But what I saw first wasn't their movement. It was the aftermath.

The Wraith had already killed the family. Children. Mother. All of them lay in pieces—burned, torn, crumpled like trash. The bodies were barely recognizable, the kind of destruction that didn't happen in combat—it happened in devotion. Ritual. Hunger.

And the Wraith was still feeding.

It held the last one—the father—by the forehead. Not the throat. Not the chest. Just the forehead, like it was pressing something holy into him, or pulling something sacred out.

The man wasn't screaming. Couldn't. His jaw was slack. His eyes rolled. The Spark was draining, and the Wraith… the Wraith trembled. Not with effort. With pleasure.

The thing trembled—rapture etched into its posture. I could barely look at it. My vision bent, reality resisting the act as if even the machine wanted to forget.

Back in civilization, a protein recovery team would've moved in by now. Bagged the corpses. Reclaimed the waste.

But here?

Here, it was just loss.

Waste.

They were supposed to process the bodies. Convert to protein. But these Wraiths weren't harvesting. They were desecrating.

Five more times I watched the same rite. Always the skull. Always the drain. Then pulp.

Dominic Voss, our lord and Savior, called it the ultimate prayer. But I'd seen prayer. And this wasn't it.



####

A Wraith emerged from the smoke, dragging a soldier by the collar. The man hung limp in its grip, bloodied but breathing—barely. His armor was scorched, one eye swollen shut, but he was still alive. Unfed on. Intact.

The Wraith had actually done good work. Taken a prisoner. Rebel, front line. Worth interrogating.

It lifted the man by the head—palm clamped over the top like it was holding a basketball. The soldier groaned.

"Where's Kat?" I barked.

The man laughed—wet, broken. He spit blood onto the Wraith's chestplate.

"You'll never get me to talk, SynRgy scum."

The Wraith tilted its head slightly. Then, without emotion, it reached down, grabbed the man's arm—and tore it from the socket.

Not a clean break. It shredded through flesh and tendon. The Wraith discarded the limb like trash.

The man screamed. High and shrill. He squirmed hard now, pain overriding dignity.

"Why the fuck did you have to do that?" I muttered.

"Noncompliant subject," the Wraith responded. Voice flat. Synthetic. Tuned to horror.

I stepped closer. The man was going to bleed out in minutes.

"Where is Kat?"

The soldier coughed. Shaking. Still smiling.

"I'll never tell you where she is."

And there it was.

She.

The rebel leader. Kat. She.

Now I knew.

The Wraith, knowing the rebel was no longer needed, began to feed. Another Spark siphoned for the promise of Heaven High.

That's when I heard it.

A whistle—sharp, high, unnatural. A breath of analog war.

Then: impact.

The arrow punched through the Wraith's cloak like the shielding didn't exist. No spark, no flash, no resistance. Just entry—clean, impossible. Straight into the core.

Old tech beat new.

And I knew what came next.

This wasn't just death. It was rupture. Core inversion. Beijing in miniature.

I ran.

No calculation. No tactics. Just the raw, stupid instinct to live.

The light was already building behind me—too fast, too bright. My boots scraped pavement slick with ash. My lungs pulled fire.

This was it. This was how it ended. Not in a duel. Not with ceremony. Just heat and pressure and silence.

No honor. No glory. Not for Voss. Not for SynRgy. Not for me.

I saw a fragment of cover—a concrete half-wall, maybe stable, maybe not—and threw myself toward it like it might still matter.

I hit the dirt a heartbeat before the Wraith detonated.

Light erased the street. Air folded inward. The blast took a city block with it. Fire, debris, bone—all gone.

My shield skin held. Just barely. Pressure slammed into my back. My ears went dead. Everything turned white.

Then black.



####

I came to slowly. Checked myself and found nothing that would stop me from completing this mission.

Kat had done this.

That's why SynRgy wanted her alive. Not because she was a symbol. Not because she was dangerous.

But because she could kill Wraiths.

And anyone who could kill Wraiths could kill whoever they wanted.

Anyone who could kill God's Wraiths could kill God.

I crawled from the crater, coughing up ash. My skin—engineered to survive drone strikes—still smoked. The dermal mesh hissed, venting residual heat through thousands of self-cauterizing capillaries.

My HUD flickered. Offline. Reboot. Pulse. Then back.

Tactical Net Reacquired.

One ping. Then eleven. Icons crawled across the far end of the map—blinking gray. My Wraiths. Still alive. Still moving.

All of them—headed south.

Toward the Port.

I pushed myself upright. The sky was fire-glow and cinders. Buildings leaned. Dust hung too thick to breathe.

A new ping. One of the Wraith signals flashed red.

Unit 07: Offline.

Gone.

A second later—another.

Unit 04: Offline.

I watched the icons vanish like lights snapped off in a hallway. Not random. Not chaos.

Targeted. Efficient. Clean.

The pattern was impossible to miss. A slow bleed toward extinction.My Wraiths were being culled.

I tapped the command feed.

Override Code: Theta-Black.

“Disengage. Fall back. Fall back. Fall—”

No response.

I sent it again. Layered in triplicate. Broadcast wide band.

Still nothing.

Their signals didn’t even flicker.

They weren’t listening.

Or worse—they heard me. And didn’t care.

I watched them keep moving. Toward the wrecked docks. Toward the ships China had abandoned when they fled. Old PLA destroyers rusted at half-mast. Civilian barges rigged as gunships. The graveyard of a failed alliance.

Beijing had paid for backing the Free States. Voss had made sure of that. A sky full of warheads and a harbor full of dead PLA ships proved it.

And now the harbor was just rot and trap.

The Wraiths didn’t know that. Or they did. And didn’t care. They were hunting her, hunting their mission. 

And then… for a second, I caught a glimpse of one of the rebels. Short, petite frame, wavy black hair, skin like shined ebony… and then, I realized just who’d they sent me after.

####

A Wraith emerged from the smoke, dragging a soldier by the collar. The man hung limp in its grip, bloodied but breathing—barely. His armor was scorched, one eye swollen shut, but he was still alive. Unfed on. Intact.

The Wraith had actually done good work. Taken a prisoner. Rebel, front line. Worth interrogating.

It lifted the man by the head—palm clamped over the top like it was holding a basketball. The soldier groaned.

"Where's Kat?" I barked.

The man laughed—wet, broken. He spit blood onto the Wraith's chestplate.

"You'll never get me to talk, SynRgy scum."

The Wraith tilted its head slightly. Then, without emotion, it reached down, grabbed the man's arm—and tore it from the socket.

Not a clean break. It shredded through flesh and tendon. The Wraith discarded the limb like trash.

The man screamed. High and shrill. He squirmed hard now, pain overriding dignity.

"Why the fuck did you have to do that?" I muttered.

"Noncompliant subject," the Wraith responded. Voice flat. Synthetic. Tuned to horror.

I stepped closer. The man was going to bleed out in minutes.

"Where is Kat?"

The soldier coughed. Shaking. Still smiling.

"I'll never tell you where she is."

And there it was.

She.

The rebel leader. Kat. She.

Now I knew.

The Wraith, knowing the rebel was no longer needed, began to feed. Another Spark siphoned for the promise of Heaven High.

That's when I heard it.

A whistle—sharp, high, unnatural. A breath of analog war.

Then: impact.

The arrow punched through the Wraith's cloak like the shielding didn't exist. No spark, no flash, no resistance. Just entry—clean, impossible. Straight into the core.

Old tech beat new.

And I knew what came next.

This wasn't just death. It was rupture. Core inversion. Beijing in miniature.

I ran.

No calculation. No tactics. Just the raw, stupid instinct to live.

The light was already building behind me—too fast, too bright. My boots scraped pavement slick with ash. My lungs pulled fire.

This was it. This was how it ended. Not in a duel. Not with ceremony. Just heat and pressure and silence.

No honor. No glory. Not for Voss. Not for SynRgy. Not for me.

I saw a fragment of cover—a concrete half-wall, maybe stable, maybe not—and threw myself toward it like it might still matter.

I hit the dirt a heartbeat before the Wraith detonated.

Light erased the street. Air folded inward. The blast took a city block with it. Fire, debris, bone—all gone.

My shield skin held. Just barely. Pressure slammed into my back. My ears went dead. Everything turned white.

Then black.



####

I came to slowly. Checked myself and found nothing that would stop me from completing this mission.

Kat had done this.

That's why SynRgy wanted her alive. Not because she was a symbol. Not because she was dangerous.

But because she could kill Wraiths.

And anyone who could kill Wraiths could kill whoever they wanted.

Anyone who could kill God's Wraiths could kill God.

I crawled from the crater, coughing up ash. My skin—engineered to survive drone strikes—still smoked. The dermal mesh hissed, venting residual heat through thousands of self-cauterizing capillaries.

My HUD flickered. Offline. Reboot. Pulse. Then back.

Tactical Net Reacquired.

One ping. Then eleven. Icons crawled across the far end of the map—blinking gray. My Wraiths. Still alive. Still moving.

All of them—headed south.

Toward the Port.

I pushed myself upright. The sky was fire-glow and cinders. Buildings leaned. Dust hung too thick to breathe.

A new ping. One of the Wraith signals flashed red.

Unit 07: Offline.

Gone.

A second later—another.

Unit 04: Offline.

I watched the icons vanish like lights snapped off in a hallway. Not random. Not chaos.

Targeted. Efficient. Clean.

The pattern was impossible to miss. A slow bleed toward extinction.My Wraiths were being culled.

I tapped the command feed.

Override Code: Theta-Black.

“Disengage. Fall back. Fall back. Fall—”

No response.

I sent it again. Layered in triplicate. Broadcast wide band.

Still nothing.

Their signals didn’t even flicker.

They weren’t listening.

Or worse—they heard me. And didn’t care.

I watched them keep moving. Toward the wrecked docks. Toward the ships China had abandoned when they fled. Old PLA destroyers rusted at half-mast. Civilian barges rigged as gunships. The graveyard of a failed alliance.

Beijing had paid for backing the Free States. Voss had made sure of that. A sky full of warheads and a harbor full of dead PLA ships proved it.

And now the harbor was just rot and trap.

The Wraiths didn’t know that. Or they did. And didn’t care. They were hunting her, hunting their mission. 

And then… for a second, I caught a glimpse of one of the rebels. Short, petite frame, wavy black hair, skin like shined ebony… and then, I realized just who’d they sent me after.
